---
title: "Symbolic Expressions and Export"
subtitle: "Inspect, convert, simplify, and evaluate pySEB expressions"
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  error: false
---

pySEB can retain a scattering expression symbolically before any physical
parameters are supplied. This tutorial selects a backend, constructs a
Gaussian-polymer form factor, exports it to several formats, converts it to a
native SymPy expression, and evaluates it in Python and C++.

Complete the [diblock tutorial](diblock-copolymer.qmd) first if pySEB is not
installed or the `World` workflow is new to you.

## Python

### 1. Select the symbolic backend

The portable backend is compiled into every standard pySEB build. List the
available backends, select `portable`, and confirm the active choice.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pyseb
import sympy

print("available:", pyseb.available_backends())
pyseb.set_backend("portable")
print("active:", pyseb.get_backend())
```

Backend selection affects construction and simplification inside C++. The
same resulting expression can still be converted to native SymPy in Python.

### 2. Construct a symbolic form factor

Add one Gaussian polymer and request its normalized form factor. No `Rg` value
is needed yet because `FormFactor` returns an expression rather than a number.

```{python}
world = pyseb.World("SymbolicTutorial")
world.Add("GaussianPolymer", "polymer")

form_factor = world.FormFactor("polymer")
print("parameters:", world.getParams())
print("expression:", form_factor)
```

The generated symbol `Rg_polymer` combines the physical parameter name with
the subunit name.

### 3. Inspect backend-level export

An `Expression` provides Python, LaTeX, and C-form renderer methods. Their
formatting capabilities depend on the active backend; the portable backend's
reliable interchange format is its Python-compatible expression string.

```{python}
print("Python-compatible:", form_factor.to_python())
print("Backend LaTeX form:", form_factor.to_latex())
print("Backend C form:", form_factor.to_cform())
```

These methods do not change `form_factor`. For fully formatted LaTeX and C from
a portable build, convert to SymPy in the next step.

### 4. Convert to native SymPy

`to_sympy` returns a normal SymPy expression. SymPy can inspect, substitute,
differentiate, simplify, or generate code from it using its own APIs.

```{python}
sympy_form_factor = pyseb.to_sympy(form_factor)

print("SymPy type:", type(sympy_form_factor).__name__)
print("operation count:", sympy.count_ops(sympy_form_factor))
print("LaTeX:", sympy.latex(sympy_form_factor))
print("C:", sympy.ccode(sympy_form_factor))
sympy_form_factor
```

This conversion is particularly useful when a larger analysis already uses
SymPy for algebra or code generation.

### 5. Substitute parameters and evaluate

Substitute the polymer radius of gyration and excess scattering length, then
create a NumPy-aware function of q. Compare it with pySEB's direct evaluator
at the same q values.

```{python}
q_symbol = sympy.Symbol("q")
parameters = {"beta_polymer": 1.0, "Rg_polymer": 5.0}
numeric_expression = sympy_form_factor.subs(parameters)
sympy_function = sympy.lambdify(q_symbol, numeric_expression, modules="numpy")

q = np.array([0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2])
from_sympy = np.asarray(sympy_function(q), dtype=float)
from_pyseb = np.asarray(
    world.EvaluateFormFactor(
        "polymer", parameters, q.tolist()
    )
)

for q_value, sympy_value, pyseb_value in zip(q, from_sympy, from_pyseb):
    print(
        f"q={q_value:g} "
        f"SymPy={sympy_value:.8f} "
        f"pySEB={pyseb_value:.8f}"
    )
```

The two columns agree because they evaluate the same symbolic form factor.

## C++

The maintained C++14 program performs the same portable-backend construction,
prints the backend-level renderers, and evaluates the expression at q = 0.1.
Use GiNaC when native C++ LaTeX/C formatting is required, or pass the portable
Python form to SymPy as demonstrated above.

```cpp
{{< include cpp/symbolic-expressions.cpp >}}
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
from _helpers import run_tutorial

run_tutorial("symbolic-expressions")
```

## Next steps

- [Compare scattering quantities](scattering-quantities.qmd)
- [Symbolic backends reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Symbolic-Backends)
- [Python API reference](https://github.com/Tobionecenobi/pySEB/wiki/Python-API-Reference)
